g.data-narration: Welcome to Cache Line Replacement Policies. We explore how cache memory decides which data to keep or evict.
When cache is full and new data arrives, we must evict existing data. The choice determines system performance.
g.text: CPU checks cache first → Hit (fast) or Miss (slow main memory fetch)

g.data-narration: Understanding cache structure: lines, sets, and associativity determine how replacement policies operate.
Least Recently Used evicts the data not accessed for the longest time, exploiting temporal locality.
g.text: Evicts the cache line that hasn't been accessed for the longest time.
Exploits temporal locality - recently used data is likely used again.

g.data-narration: First In First Out evicts the oldest cache line regardless of recent access. Simple but can cause anomalies.
g.text: Evicts the oldest cache line based on insertion time only.
Does NOT consider if data was recently accessed - treats cache as a queue.

g.data-narration: Least Frequently Used tracks access count and evicts the least accessed line. Good for uneven access patterns.
g.text: Evicts the cache line with the lowest access frequency counter.
Good for workloads with non-uniform access patterns.

g.data-narration: Random replacement is simplest but unpredictable. Optimal is theoretically perfect but impossible to implement.
g.text: Evicts line used furthest in future

g.data-narration: Let's trace LRU with a 2-way cache. Reference string: A,B,C,D,A,B,E shows how LRU evicts oldest accessed.
g.text: 2-way set-associative cache, 1 set. Reference: A, B, C, D, A, B, E

g.data-narration: Compare FIFO and LRU with cache capacity 3. Reference: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 shows different behavior.
Belady's Anomaly: FIFO can have WORSE hit rate with MORE cache slots. This counterintuitive phenomenon puzzles many.
g.text: With FIFO, increasing cache size can DECREASE hit ratio!

g.data-narration: Hardware complexity increases from Random (simplest) to LRU (most complex). Microcontrollers often prefer simpler policies.
Average Memory Access Time formula combines hit time, miss rate, and miss penalty to measure cache performance.
g.text: Key Formula: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)
AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)

g.data-narration: Real processors use Pseudo-LRU to approximate LRU with less hardware complexity using binary tree structures.
g.text: Real processors use tree-based Pseudo-LRU to approximate LRU with less hardware.

g.data-narration: In summary: LRU best practical, FIFO simple but anomalous, Optimal theoretical benchmark. Policy choice impacts microcontroller performance.
g.text: • LRU: Best practical performance, exploits temporal locality
• FIFO: Simple but suffers Belady's Anomaly
• LFU: Good for skewed access patterns

g.data-narration: For exams: Practice tracing reference strings, memorize complexity order, and remember LRU exploits temporal locality.
g.text: 1. Practice tracing reference strings 2. Remember: Random→FIFO→LFU→LRU (simplest to most complex)
3. LRU exploits temporal locality 4. Belady's Anomaly only occurs with FIFO (not LRU) 5. Optimal is theoretical benchmark
